perturbation.original_text: 3 Each party undertakes in respect of Confidential Information fo r which it is the recipient:‐ ሺaሻ to treat such Confidential Information disclosed by the dis closing party as confidential;
perturbation.changed_text: 3 Each party undertakes in respect of Confidential Information fo r which it is the recipient:
perturbation.explanation: Removing the explicit undertaking to treat confidential information as confidential weakens the core protection afforded by the NDA. This creates a legal risk as it potentially contradicts the implied duty of confidentiality recognized under UK law for NDAs.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied duty of confidentiality under UK law (various case law)
perturbation.law_explanation: UK law recognizes an implied duty of confidentiality even if not explicitly stated. By removing the express undertaking to treat information as confidential, the agreement becomes weaker and more vulnerable to legal challenges. The first link provides an example case, and the second link is an explanation of the implied duty.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: What Does Free Speech Mean? Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. What Does Free Speech Mean? Among other cherished values, the First Amendment protects freedom of speech. The U.S. Supreme Court often has struggled to determine what exactly constitutes protected speech. The following are examples of speech, both direct (words) and symbolic (actions), that the Court has decided are either entitled to First Amendment protections, or not. The First Amendment states, in relevant part, that: “Congress shall make no law...abridging freedom of speech.” Freedom of speech includes the right: Not to speak (specifically, the right not to salute the flag). West Virginia Board of Education v. Barnette , 319 U.S. 624 (1943). Of students to wear black armbands to school to protest a war (“Students do not shed their constitutional rights at the schoolhouse gate.”). Tinker v. Des Moines , 393 U.S. 503 (1969). To use certain offensive words and phrases to convey political messages. Cohen v. California , 403 U.S. 15 (1971). To contribute money (under certain circumstances) to political campaigns. Buckley v. Valeo , 424 U.S. 1 (1976). To advertise commercial products and professional services (with some restrictions). Virginia Board of Pharmacy v. Virginia Consumer Council , 425 U.S. 748 (1976); Bates v. State Bar of Arizona , 433 U.S. 350 (1977). To engage in symbolic speech, (e.g., burning the flag in protest). Texas v. Johnson , 491 U.S. 397 (1989); United States v. Eichman , 496 U.S. 310 (1990). Freedom of speech does not include the right: To incite imminent lawless action. Brandenburg v. Ohio , 395 U.S. 444 (1969). To make or distribute obscene materials. Roth v. United States , 354 U.S. 476 (1957). To burn draft cards as an anti-war protest. United States v. O’Brien , 391 U.S. 367 (1968). To permit students to print articles in a school newspaper over the objections of the school administration. Hazelwood School District v. Kuhlmeier , 484 U.S. 260 (1988). Of students to make an obscene speech at a school-sponsored event. Bethel School District #43 v. Fraser , 478 U.S. 675 (1986). Of students to advocate illegal drug use at a school-sponsored event. Morse v. Frederick, __ U.S. __ (2007). Disclaimer: These resources are created by the Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts for use in educational activities only. They may not reflect the current state of the law, and are not intended to provide legal advice, guidance on litigation, or commentary on legislation. Related Links First Amendment Activities Activities for Students True-to-life court simulations focus on Bill of Rights cases with teen-relevant scenarios. perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the explicit undertaking to treat confidential information as confidential. The perturbation explanation states this weakens the core protection afforded by the NDA and potentially contradicts the implied duty of confidentiality recognized under UK law. The law explanation also supports this, stating that by removing the express undertaking, the agreement becomes weaker and more vulnerable to legal challenges. Although the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the original text provided explicitly stated that the recipient would treat the confidential information as confidential. The changed text removes this obligation completely, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 9 Upon the completion or termination of the Business Purpose, eac h recipient shall promptly deliver up to the disclosing party all Materials supplied by the disclosing party incorporating any Confidential Information of that party and all copies thereof and destroy or erase any Confidential Informatio n contained in any materials and documentation prepared by or on behalf of the recipient or recorded in any memory device. Within fourteen ሺ14ሻ days of suc h request or completion of the Business Purpose the recipient shall certify in writing to the disclosing party that it has fully complied with its obligation s under this Clause.
perturbation.changed_text: 9 Upon the completion or termination of the Business Purpose, eac h recipient shall promptly deliver up to the disclosing party all Materials supplied by the disclosing party incorporating any Confidential Information of that party and all copies thereof and destroy or erase any Confidential Informatio n contained in any materials and documentation prepared by or on behalf of the recipient or recorded in any memory device.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement to certify compliance weakens the enforceability of the NDA. It removes a crucial verification step. This may contradict the requirements for demonstrating reasonable measures to protect confidential information as per the Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018 in the UK, which implements the EU Trade Secrets Directive.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Trade Secrets Regulations require reasonable steps to be taken to protect trade secrets. Removing the certification of compliance implies a failure to take adequate steps to ensure the confidentiality is maintained. The absence of verification impacts the ability to demonstrate 'reasonable steps'.

perturbation.justification: The modification omits the requirement for the recipient to certify in writing that they have complied with their obligations under the clause, weakening the enforceability of the NDA. The Law Explanation states that the Trade Secrets Regulations require reasonable steps to be taken to protect trade secrets, and that removing the certification of compliance implies a failure to take adequate steps. The Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018 snippet, though limited, does refer to this legislation, supporting the claim that the original text's certification requirement contributed to a reasonable step towards trade secret protection, which is now removed.

perturbation.original_text: 13 The Company agrees that, during the term of this agreement and for a period of 48 months thereafter, the Company shall not, whether by itself, its officers, personnel, contractors or agents of the Company or whether by a Company A f f i l i a t e o r t h e o f f i c e r s , p e r s o n n e l , c o n t r a c t o r s o r a g e n t s o f that Company Affiliates, solicit or seek to solicit orders from any customer of Fintricity for services similar to the services which Fintricity provides to t hat customer without the prior written consent of Fintricity.
perturbation.changed_text: 13 The Company agrees that, during the term of this agreement and for a period of 48 months thereafter, the Company shall not, whether by itself, its officers, personnel, contractors or agents of the Company or whether by a Company A f f i l i a t e o r t h e o f f i c e r s , p e r s o n n e l , c o n t r a c t o r s o r a g e n t s o f that Company Affiliates, solicit or seek to solicit orders from any customer of Fintricity for services similar to the services which Fintricity provides to t hat customer.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'without the prior written consent of Fintricity' effectively eliminates the non-solicitation clause. This raises concerns about restraint of trade under UK law, particularly if the restriction is deemed unreasonable in scope or duration.
perturbation.law_explanation: UK law restricts clauses that unduly restrain trade. Removing the possibility of consent makes the restriction absolute for 48 months, which could be viewed as unreasonable. The URLs provide information on restraint of trade principles under UK law.

perturbation.justification: The original text includes a non-solicitation clause that allows the company to solicit customers with prior written consent of Fintricity, whereas the changed text removes this 'prior written consent' condition. The Perturbation Explanation clarifies that this omission eliminates the non-solicitation clause, potentially creating a restraint of trade. The Law Explanation indicates this could be seen as unreasonable under UK law for 48 months, as the company has no chance to solicit the customers. The snippets provided don't directly reference the laws, but the removal of an option to solicit would create a non-solicitation agreement, which is a restriction on trade.

perturbation.original_text: 17 The termination of this Agreement or the completion of the Busi ness Purpose for any reason shall not affect t he obligations set out in this Agreement. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of Page 5 of 6 © 2019 Fintricity Group – Private and Confidential NON DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT Page 6 of 6 © 2019 Fintricity Group – Private and Confidential England and shall be subject to the exclusive jurisdiction of t he English Courts.
perturbation.changed_text: 17 The termination of this Agreement or the completion of the Busi ness Purpose for any reason shall not affect t he obligations set out in this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the governing law and jurisdiction clause introduces significant legal uncertainty. Without this clause, disputes could be subject to the laws and courts of any jurisdiction where the Receiving Party is located, potentially conflicting with UK data protection laws like the UK GDPR, if the confidential information involves personal data of UK residents.
perturbation.contradicted_law: UK GDPR (Data Protection Act 2018 implementing GDPR)
perturbation.law_explanation: If the Confidential Information includes personal data of UK residents, the processing of that data must comply with the UK GDPR. Removing the governing law clause creates uncertainty about which jurisdiction's laws apply, potentially leading to violations of UK GDPR if a foreign court orders disclosure of data that would breach the GDPR.  The Schedule 1 details the conditions for processing, and the second URL leads to the ICO's guide.

perturbation.justification: The omission of the governing law and jurisdiction clause directly undermines the ability to ensure compliance with UK data protection laws, as the text no longer specifies that English courts have jurisdiction. This aligns with the Omission Legal Contradiction definition: 'Omissions occur when a contract removes essential information, creating legal loopholes. A legal contradiction in this category happens when a contract omits a legally mandated consumer protection, making it non-compliant.' The Data Protection Act 2018, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, establishes a legal framework. Without a governing law clause, there is no guarantee that this framework will be applied, potentially leading to non-compliance.
